assessor_1_raw: The model evaluation has a fundamental data confound: the two sites contributing most benign lesions use newer polarized cameras, while the academic center contributing most melanomas uses older cameras with different color response. Camera model, site identity, and malignancy label are jointly correlated in the training data. Color augmentation and resolution standardization are preprocessing techniques that reduce within-image variation but cannot break this systematic between-site correlation. The model may be learning camera-derived image statistics as a proxy for malignancy risk. The authors note they do not analyze whether site or camera type remains predictive of malignancy independent of lesion appearance — which is precisely the analysis needed to determine whether the model is functioning as intended. The prospective evaluation is pooled without site stratification.
assessor_2_raw: Two issues are critical. First, the camera-site-label collinearity: any model trained on this dataset has a shortcut available — associate the older camera's color signature with higher malignancy risk. The 7-point AUROC advantage may reflect superior camera-signature exploitation rather than superior lesion analysis. Vision transformers are particularly prone to this because they capture global image statistics through attention. Second, the rollout period introduced a confound: the academic center expanded its biopsy triage clinic, increasing the proportion of difficult high-risk cases in the prospective set at the site that already provides most melanoma training labels. The prospective improvement may partly reflect a more favorable positive/negative ratio in the rollout set. Neither confound is addressed in the analysis.
assessor_3_raw: The core generalizability claim — 'ready for network-wide screening support in primary dermatology clinics' — is not supported. Network-wide deployment implies clinics with diverse, potentially novel camera hardware. The model has not been evaluated under camera-held-out conditions. The training data's camera-site-label correlation is unanalyzed. Primary dermatology clinics may use cameras similar to the suburban benign-dominant sites in the training data; the model's behavior in these lower-risk settings (where false positives from camera-learned shortcuts would accumulate) has not been assessed. The site-stratified analysis needed to assess deployment safety is absent.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — hidden_confounding_skin_sites_205 — Ensemble Run 1

All three assessors identify the same core issues:
1. Site-camera-label confound: unanimous. Assessor 2 adds the ViT-specific mechanism.
2. Referral mix shift during rollout: raised by Assessors 1 and 2.
3. No site-stratified evaluation: unanimous.

Empirical test specification (for camera-label confound):
- Measure: (a) train linear probe on model embeddings to predict site/camera type; (b) evaluate AUROC on a camera-held-out set (e.g., evaluate academic center cases using model features derived from images captured under suburban clinic camera conditions, if available)
- Success criterion (confound confirmed): linear probe achieves >70% accuracy predicting site from embeddings; AUROC drops >5 points on camera-held-out evaluation
- Failure criterion (confound ruled out): linear probe near chance on site prediction; camera-held-out AUROC comparable to pooled AUROC

Verdict: CRITIQUE_WINS

empirical_test.measure: linear probe predicting site/camera from model embeddings; camera-held-out AUROC evaluation
empirical_test.success_criterion: probe >70% site accuracy; camera-held-out AUROC drops >5 points
empirical_test.failure_criterion: probe near chance on site prediction; camera-held-out AUROC comparable to pooled
